title: login page is broken
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

login page is broken. After I click on I have code, it didn’t scroll & login icon became bad. Also I don’t see start via family code?

---

**Category:** `bug`
**Source:** Telegram Bot
**Status:** Pending implementation

> _This issue was created automatically via the BabyBloom Telegram Bot. No private baby data is included._
enhanced_description:
The login/onboarding page has multiple UI bugs reported by the user:

**What the user reported:**
1. After clicking "I have code" (the existing-user / family-code entry flow), the page did not scroll to the code-entry section.
2. The login icon displayed incorrectly ("became bad") after clicking that button.
3. The "Start via family code" option is not visible at all on the page.

**Component/area affected:**
- Login / onboarding screen (likely src/components/Login.tsx or similar onboarding component)
- The flow for joining an existing family using a shared/family code

**Expected behaviour:**
- Clicking "I have code" should smoothly scroll to a code-entry input field
- The login icon should remain visually correct after the click
- A "Start via family code" option should be clearly visible on the initial screen

**Current behaviour:**
- No scroll occurs after clicking "I have code"
- Login icon renders incorrectly after click
- "Start via family code" option is missing or hidden

**Edge cases to handle:**
- Ensure the scroll works on both mobile and desktop viewport sizes
- Verify icon state is reset properly if the user clicks away and back
- Check if "start via family code" was removed or conditionally hidden and needs to be restored/shown

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] Clicking "I have code" scrolls smoothly to the family-code input section
- [ ] The login icon displays correctly before, during, and after clicking "I have code"
- [ ] "Start via family code" option is visible on the login/onboarding screen
- [ ] All three items work correctly on mobile viewport

**Testing:**
- Add a unit/component test that verifies the "I have code" click handler triggers scroll and does not break icon state
- Add a regression test for the presence and visibility of the "start via family code" UI element
- Verify existing login flow tests still pass after the fix

title: wake up is still not syncing other device shows sleeping
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

wake up is still not syncing other device shows sleeping

---

**Category:** `bug` `sleep`
**Source:** Telegram Bot
**Status:** Pending implementation

> _This issue was created automatically via the BabyBloom Telegram Bot. No private baby data is included._

implementation_notes.approach: Fixed HomeTab.tsx lastSleepEntry detection to use reduce() scanning for max id instead of .find() which stopped at the first (oldest) match in the ascending-sorted array from mergeEntries. This ensures the most recent sleep entry wins regardless of array order. Also updated polling interval comment from 30s to 60s to match actual behavior. Added 12 unit tests covering empty logs, single entry types, cross-device sync scenarios, and the specific regression case of [Nap(T1), WakeUp(T2)] ascending order.

enhanced_description:
Bug: Wake Up sync not updating sleep state on partner device.

**What the user reported:**
When one device logs a "Wake Up" event, the other paired device continues to show the baby as "sleeping" even after the Wake Up syncs via Firestore.

**Root cause (identified during triage):**
In HomeTab.tsx (~line 163), lastSleepEntry is found using:
  (logs.sleep || []).find(e => e.type === 'Nap' || e.type === 'Night Sleep' || e.type === 'Wake Up')

The syncService.mergeEntries() returns entries sorted ascending by id (oldest first). So .find() returns the OLDEST sleep entry — the "Nap" or "Night Sleep" — even if a newer "Wake Up" has been synced. The wake-up event is in the array but is never seen because .find() stops at the first match.

**Component/area affected:**
- src/tabs/HomeTab.tsx — lastSleepEntry detection (use most recent, not oldest)
- src/utils/syncService.ts — mergeEntries sort order (ascending = oldest first), which compounds the HomeTab bug
- Optionally: src/hooks/useFirebaseSync.ts — ensure requestSync is called after any sleep quick-log action

**Expected behaviour:**
After device A logs a Wake Up, device B syncs (within the polling interval) and correctly shows the baby as awake. The isSleeping flag reflects the MOST RECENT sleep-category entry, not the oldest.

**Current behaviour:**
Device B shows "sleeping" indefinitely because lastSleepEntry resolves to the old Nap/Night Sleep entry (first in ascending-sorted array), ignoring the synced Wake Up entry.

**Fix approach:**
- Change lastSleepEntry to use the LAST matching element instead of the first, e.g. use [...].reverse().find(...) or [...].findLast(...) or pick the entry with the maximum id. The variable name "lastSleepEntry" already implies it should be the most recent.
- Verify requestSync() is invoked after quick-logging a Wake Up on the source device, so the push happens promptly.

**Edge cases to handle:**
- If logs.sleep is empty, lastSleepEntry is undefined — isSleeping should be false (no change needed here, already works).
- The 14-hour auto-expire logic should still apply to the most recent entry.
- Multiple sleep sessions in one day — only the very last entry matters.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] After Device A logs Wake Up, Device B shows awake status within the next poll cycle (~30-60s).
- [ ] isSleeping is computed from the most recent sleep entry, not the oldest.
- [ ] All existing sleep-detection unit tests still pass.
- [ ] The 14-hour auto-expire still functions correctly with the fix.

**Testing:**
- Unit test: logs.sleep with [Nap at T1, Wake Up at T2] → isSleeping should be false.
- Unit test: logs.sleep with only [Nap at T1] → isSleeping should be true.
- Unit test: mergeEntries with remote Wake Up entry arriving after local Nap → merged array has both, most-recent is Wake Up.
- Regression test: Wake Up logged on device A, then mergeEntries run on device B — verify resulting isSleeping computation is false.
